serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of sweet and sour sotong is approximately 250 g, comprising stir-fried squid pieces coated in sweet and sour sauce with vegetables, served as a shared side dish or individual main.

nutrient_source.title: Cuttlefish / Squid stir-fried with sauce — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'sweet and sour sotong' exists in MyFCD. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD entries for stir-fried squid with sauce and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA squid dish data. Sugar and ketchup contribution to carbohydrate and sodium are factored in based on standard recipe proportions. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood dishes with sweet sauce — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweet and sour sauce dishes and stir-fried squid
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet and sour sotong. GI estimated at 51 (low) based on analogous sweet-and-sour sauce dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 45–55 for stir-fried dishes with tomato-ketchup-based sweet and sour sauces. The squid protein component lowers GI relative to pure carbohydrate sources. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 19.5) / 100 ≈ 10.1, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 7.8 × 2.5 = 19.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1200 mg per serving) from soy sauce, ketchup, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sweet and sour sotong to small portions infrequently, as a single restaurant serving can contribute approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or choosing lower-sodium soy sauce variants may help mitigate risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (51) but moderate glycemic load (~10 per serving) driven by added sugar and ketchup in the sweet and sour sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that despite its low glycemic index of 51, the sweet and sour sauce contributes approximately 5.2 g of sugar per 100 g; consuming a standard 250 g portion delivers roughly 13 g of added sugar, which can raise postprandial blood glucose, and portion control combined with pairing with low-GI vegetables is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol content (~145 mg per 100 g) from squid, partially offset by low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, moderate consumption of sweet and sour sotong is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet, though the high sodium from the sauce warrants caution and the dish should not be consumed daily.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet and Sour Sotong is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean seafood dish featuring squid (sotong) stir-fried in a vibrant sweet and sour sauce. The dish is prepared by wok-frying cleaned squid rings or whole baby squid at high heat, then coating them in a sauce made from tomato ketchup, rice vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, garlic, and ginger, often with the addition of onion, bell pepper, and pineapple chunks for texture and flavour contrast. The result is tender, slightly chewy squid with a glossy, tangy-sweet sauce that balances acidity from vinegar and ketchup with sweetness from sugar and pineapple. The dish is commonly served as a shared side dish in Malaysian Chinese restaurants, Indian-Muslim eateries, and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, sweet and sour sotong provides a moderate protein source (~11.5 g per 100 g) from squid, which is naturally low in fat but high in dietary cholesterol. The sauce contributes significant sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) and added sugar (~5.2 g per 100 g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per standard serving is low (~10), driven by the sugar-rich sauce rather than the squid itself, despite its low glycemic index of 51. Squid is a good source of vitamin B12, phosphorus, and selenium, supporting neurological function and bone health. The dish is halal-certified in most Malaysian and Singaporean establishments and is dairy-free, though it contains mollusc allergens and gluten from soy sauce.

lunch or dinner main with steamed rice

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sweet and sour sotong exists in IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from stir-fried squid with sauce entries in MyFCD and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (squid) is well-characterised in approved sources and the sauce composition is standard and well-documented.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried squid with sauce entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA; no exact 'sweet and sour sotong' entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries for squid dishes. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA source entries for this dish category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD source for this dish; selenium data for squid is available in USDA but was not used as primary source for this Malaysian dish. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as squid and vegetable oil used in preparation do not contribute measurable trans fats; confirmed negligible per FAO/INFOODS SEA data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (51, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database sweet-and-sour sauce dish analogues; no direct GI measurement for sweet and sour sotong was found in the literature. 7. The input food_group 'Grains and staples' has been corrected to 'Fish and seafood' as sotong is a cephalopod seafood dish. 8. Cuisine tags from input (Noodle, Fried Rice, Roti Prata) are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded from the output. 9. Halal status is assumed based on typical Malaysian restaurant preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 10. The aliases_by_language entry 'மீठा اور खट्टा स्क्विड' (Hindi) appears verbatim in the flat aliases list as required by the aliases consistency rule.
